era_context: The War of Austrian Succession (1740–1748) dominates European politics; the Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi and Yongzheng successors consolidates control over China and Central Asia; the British Empire expands colonial holdings in North America and India; the Ottoman Empire faces internal fragmentation and external pressure from Russia and Austria.

events.title: Battle of Culloden
events.description: On 16 April 1746, the Jacobite rising of 1745 was decisively crushed at Culloden Moor near Inverness. The Highland army of Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) was routed by the Duke of Cumberland's government forces. This marked the effective end of the Stuart restoration movement and the beginning of systematic suppression of Highland clan culture, including the Disarming Acts and the end of heritable jurisdictions. The battle had profound consequences for Scottish society, accelerating the integration of the Highlands into the British state and the decline of Gaelic culture.
key_figures: William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland (British commander)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by eyewitnesses and military records; The Lyon in Mourning (Robert Forbes, compiled 1747–1775)

events.certainty_note: Date and outcome are well-documented in contemporary military records and eyewitness accounts.

events.title: Heritable Jurisdictions Act (Scotland)
events.description: Following the Jacobite defeat, the British Parliament passed the Heritable Jurisdictions Act in 1746, abolishing the feudal right of Scottish landowners to hold judicial authority over their lands. This was a direct consequence of the rebellion and represented a major centralisation of state power. The act effectively ended the quasi-independent power of Highland chiefs and integrated Scottish law more fully under Westminster control. Compensation was offered to affected landowners, but the symbolic and practical impact was profound.
key_figures: Henry Pelham (First Lord of the Treasury)

sources.name: Parliamentary records and the text of the Heritable Jurisdictions Act, 1746

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary legislation is precisely dated and documented.

events.title: Battle of Rocoux (War of Austrian Succession)
events.description: On 11 October 1746, French forces under Marshal Saxe defeated the Allied army (British, Dutch, Austrian) commanded by the Duke of Cumberland at Rocoux in the Spanish Netherlands. This was a significant French victory in the ongoing War of Austrian Succession, consolidating French military dominance in the Low Countries and contributing to the eventual French territorial gains in the peace settlement. The battle demonstrated the effectiveness of Saxe's tactical innovations and the relative weakness of the Allied coalition.
key_figures: William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland (Allied commander)

sources.name: Contemporary military dispatches and memoirs; Saxe's Reveries upon the Art of War
Historiographic consensus from military histories of the War of Austrian Succession

events.certainty_note: Date and outcome are well-documented in military records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Qing consolidation of Tibet
events.description: In 1746, the Qing Dynasty under the Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735, but policies continued under Qianlong, r. 1735–1796) further consolidated control over Tibet following the 1728 establishment of the Amban (imperial resident) system. By 1746, the Qing had effectively subordinated the Tibetan government to imperial oversight, though maintaining the Dalai Lama as a figurehead. This represented a major expansion of Qing territorial control and the beginning of systematic Chinese administrative integration of Tibet, which would have long-term consequences for Sino-Tibetan relations.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation process occurred over several years; 1746 is approximate for the completion of administrative integration.

events.title: Nadir Shah's invasion of the Caucasus
events.description: Nadir Shah of Persia, having consolidated power and defeated the Ottomans, turned his attention to the Caucasus in 1746, launching campaigns to bring the region under Persian control. These campaigns were part of Nadir Shah's broader imperial expansion and represented a reassertion of Persian power in the region after Ottoman and Russian encroachments. The campaigns were largely successful in the short term but contributed to the destabilisation of the Caucasus that would persist for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The campaigns occurred in this period; exact dating of specific operations is less certain.

events.title: Founding of Princeton College
events.description: The College of New Jersey (later Princeton University) was formally chartered on 22 November 1746 by royal charter from Governor Jonathan Belcher. The college was founded as a Presbyterian institution to train ministers and was initially located in Elizabeth, New Jersey, before moving to Princeton in 1756. This was part of the broader expansion of higher education in the American colonies and reflected the growing intellectual and religious sophistication of colonial society. The college would become one of the most influential institutions in American history.
events.certainty_note: The charter date is precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Culloden as a 'turning point' in Highland history has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. While the battle was decisive militarily, recent historians argue that the decline of Highland clan culture was a longer process than the 'immediate suppression' narrative suggests, with economic and social changes predating and postdating the battle. Additionally, the role of the Duke of Cumberland in 'pacifying' the Highlands is contested; some scholars emphasise that the Highlands were already economically integrated into the British system before 1746.
historiographic_note: 1746 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for Britain, France, and the War of Austrian Succession. Sources are predominantly from the perspective of European powers and colonial elites. Non-European regions (Asia, Africa, the Americas outside British North America) are less thoroughly documented in Western archives. The Qing consolidation of Tibet is well-attested in Chinese sources but less visible in European records. Indigenous perspectives on colonial expansion in North America are largely absent from contemporary European documentation.

graph_edges.note: Culloden defeat prompted the Heritable Jurisdictions Act
Part of the War of Austrian Succession, leading to the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle
